1980s.title: Hydropower economy begins scaling
1980s.body: Bhutan's price story is tied to a small import-dependent economy, the ngultrum's link to the Indian rupee, and early hydropower-led development.
1980s.driver: Imported goods, food, Indian price spillovers
1980s.outcome: Inflation moved through trade links as much as domestic demand.
1980s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

1990s.title: Small-market prices and regional trade
1990s.body: Limited domestic production, road access, and regional trade made consumer prices sensitive to food and imported goods.
1990s.event: Expansion of public services and regional trade links
1990s.outcome: Purchasing power changed gradually but remained exposed to imported inflation.

2000s.title: Democratic transition and commodity shock
2000s.body: Bhutan's 2008 democratic transition coincided with global food and fuel pressure, bringing external price shocks into household budgets.
2000s.event: 2008 democratic transition and global commodity spike
2000s.outcome: The price index rose as global shocks reached local markets.
2000s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

2010s.title: Credit, imports, and rupee-link pressure
2010s.body: Credit growth, imported goods, and the India trade link shaped inflation as Bhutan balanced modernization with external-account constraints.
2010s.outcome: A small economy's purchasing power stayed tightly linked to regional price movements.

2020s.title: Pandemic reopening and import costs
2020s.body: Tourism disruption, reopening, fuel costs, and imported goods shaped household purchasing power in the early 2020s.
2020s.outcome: The 2024 endpoint captures post-pandemic price pressure in a highly open small economy.
